title: Ink on Polyester
stainChemistry: Classification: Dye (synthetic colorant). Ballpoint ink contains dye (Crystal Violet or eosin) dissolved in an oil-based vehicle (phenoxyethanol, benzyl alcohol) with resin binder. The resin anchors dye to fiber at a molecular level. Gel pen ink uses suspended pigment particles. Synthetic dyes bond to polyester fibers via covalent bonds or physical entrapment. Treatment: solvent to dissolve dye carrier, followed by oxidizing or reducing bleach to cleave chromophore bonds. Polyester is chemically resistant to most spotting agents.
whyThisWorks: Ink is a dye in an oil-based carrier designed to permanently mark surfaces. Ink sits on polyester's surface rather than absorbing in deeply. Alcohol or solvent dissolves the carrier holding the dye in place, letting you lift it away before it bonds permanently.
defaultAssumption: Treat as unknown age / possibly dried.

steps: 1. Apply VDS (Volatile Dry Solvent) to dissolve the dye pigment and its binder. VDS is gentle but effective — it lifts color molecules from the fiber. Blot with clean cloth, replacing often as it picks up dye. Work from outside in.

2. If VDS plateaus, switch to amyl acetate for stronger solvent action. This targets dye molecules that have bonded more deeply to the fiber. Test on a hidden area first. Never use on acetate fabrics.
3. Flush with NSD to remove all solvent residue and any remaining pigment particles. Rinse thoroughly with cool water.
professionalProtocol.temperature: ambient — avoid high heat; polyester melts above 250°C and heat can set dye permanently
warnings: Rubbing instead of blotting — spreads the stain
Not testing in an inconspicuous area first
Using too aggressive a chemical without checking surface compatibility

steps: 1. Dab the stain with a cloth dipped in IPA (IPA) (isopropyl 70%).
2. Work from the outside edge toward the center. Swap to a clean part of the cloth often.
3. Keep dabbing until no more color transfers to the cloth.
5. Air dry — don't use a dryer until the stain is gone.
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Try acetone (test first) or a professional dry cleaning solvent. If the stain survives two rounds, a professional cleaner can use concentrated agents and specialized equipment.
warnings: Always blot — never rub. Rubbing pushes the stain deeper into the fabric.
Use a white cloth to avoid transferring dye from a colored cloth.
IPA (IPA) works on most dye stains because it dissolves the carrier that holds the pigment.

neverDo: Apply heat before the dye is fully removed — heat polymerizes dye and makes it permanent
Rub instead of blot — spreads the dye further
fiberSensitivities: Polyester is chemically resistant to most spotting agents. Avoid high heat (melts above 250°C). Safe for enzyme, oxidizer, and solvent treatment.

escalation.when: VDS plateau is reached and dye has covalently bonded to the fiber
escalation.whatToTell: Ink stain on polyester, dye-based. Treated at home with IPA (IPA). Please use professional solvent protocol.

contextQuestions: Is this ballpoint, gel pen, or marker ink?
Is the stain fresh or has it been sitting?
What color is the polyester — white, light, or dark?
Has the garment been through the dryer?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Gel pen ink uses pigment particles that may respond differently than ballpoint dye. Dryer exposure can polymerize dye into the fiber permanently. Fresh stains have much better outcomes. On light-colored polyester, any residual tinting may be more visible.
